questions.text: The classical Mendel’s work on his ‘laws of inheritance’ was performed on the organism
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Garden pea**

Gregor Mendel conducted his famous genetics experiments on **Pisum sativum (garden pea)**.

Why pea plant?
• It shows **clear contrasting characters**.
• It has **short life cycle**.
• Self-pollination occurs naturally but cross-pollination can also be done easily.

Option Analysis:
• Drosophila → Used by Thomas Hunt Morgan for chromosome theory.
• Snapdragon → Used for incomplete dominance experiments.
• Garden pea → Correct; Mendel derived the **Law of Segregation** and **Law of Independent Assortment**.
• Sweet pea → Used later in genetic linkage studies.

questions.text: The sugar present in DNA is
questions.solution:
This question is from **basic molecular biology / nucleic acids**, a common topic in Biology for **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**The sugar present in DNA is**

A. Glucose
B. Fructose
C. Deoxyribose
D. Ribose

✅ **Correct Answer: C. Deoxyribose**

---

# Core Concept

DNA stands for **Deoxyribonucleic acid**.

Each unit of DNA (nucleotide) contains **three components**:

1. **Nitrogenous base**
2. **Phosphate group**
3. **Pentose sugar (Deoxyribose)**

Thus the sugar present in DNA is **deoxyribose**.

Structure:

[
\text{DNA nucleotide} = \text{Base} + \text{Deoxyribose sugar} + \text{Phosphate}
]

---

# DNA vs RNA Sugar

| Molecule | Sugar Present |
|---|---|
| DNA | Deoxyribose |
| RNA | Ribose |

The difference is that **deoxyribose has one oxygen atom less** than ribose.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Glucose ❌ (Incorrect)

**Glucose** is a **six-carbon sugar (hexose)** used as a **source of energy in cells**.

Example:

• Blood sugar
• Cellular respiration

It is **not part of DNA structure**.

---

## B. Fructose ❌ (Incorrect)

**Fructose** is also a **hexose sugar**, commonly called **fruit sugar**.

It is found in:

• Fruits
• Honey

It is **not present in nucleic acids**.

---

## C. Deoxyribose ✔ (Correct)

**Deoxyribose** is a **five-carbon sugar (pentose sugar)**.

It forms part of the **DNA backbone** along with phosphate groups.

Thus this is the **correct answer**.

---

## D. Ribose ❌ (Incorrect)

**Ribose** is the sugar present in **Ribonucleic acid (RNA)**.

Thus ribose is associated with **RNA, not DNA**.

---

# Quick Comparison

| Molecule | Sugar |
|---|---|
| DNA | Deoxyribose |
| RNA | Ribose |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember the name:

• **DNA → Deoxy-ribose**
• **RNA → Ribose**

So the sugar in DNA is **Deoxyribose**.

questions.text: Hereditary symptoms are moved in the descendant of creatures by
questions.solution:
This question is from **genetics and cell biology**, a common topic in the Biology section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Hereditary symptoms (traits) are moved in the descendants of creatures by**

A. Ribosome
B. Chromosome
C. Plasma
D. Lysosome

✅ **Correct Answer: B. Chromosome**

---

# Core Concept

**Hereditary traits** are characteristics passed from **parents to offspring**.

These traits are controlled by **genes**, which are located on **Chromosome**.

Structure:

[
\text{Chromosome} = \text{DNA} + \text{Proteins}
]

Genes present on chromosomes carry **genetic information** responsible for traits such as:

• Eye color
• Height
• Blood group

Thus hereditary information is transmitted through **chromosomes**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Ribosome ❌ (Incorrect)

A **ribosome** is the cell organelle responsible for **protein synthesis**.

Function:

• Converts genetic information from RNA into **proteins**.

However, it **does not carry hereditary information**.

---

## B. Chromosome ✔ (Correct)

Chromosomes contain **DNA and genes**.

During reproduction:

• Chromosomes are passed from **parents to offspring**.

Thus hereditary traits are transmitted via **chromosomes**.

---

## C. Plasma ❌ (Incorrect)

Plasma refers to **cell cytoplasm or blood plasma**, which mainly transports substances.

It does **not store genetic information**.

---

## D. Lysosome ❌ (Incorrect)

A **lysosome** is known as the **“suicide bag” of the cell**.

Function:

• Breaks down waste materials
• Digests damaged cell components

It has **no role in heredity**.

---

# Quick Summary

| Cell Structure | Function |
|---|---|
| Chromosome | Carries genes and hereditary traits |
| Ribosome | Protein synthesis |
| Lysosome | Digestion of cell waste |
| Plasma | Fluid transport |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember:

[
\text{Genes} \rightarrow \text{Chromosomes} \rightarrow \text{Heredity}
]

So hereditary traits are transmitted through **chromosomes**.

questions.text: Which of the following is responsible for controlling hereditary characters of the living cells?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: None of the above**

Hereditary traits are controlled by **genes present on DNA molecules**.

Option Analysis:
• Enzyme → Biological catalysts.
• Hormone → Chemical messenger regulating physiology.
• RNA → Helps in protein synthesis but does not store hereditary information.
• None of the above → Correct because hereditary information is stored in **DNA**.

questions.text: The unit of heredity in our body is called
questions.solution:
This question is from **genetics**, a very common topic in Biology asked in **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**The unit of heredity in our body is called**

A. Chromosome
B. DNA
C. Gene
D. Nucleus

✅ **Correct Answer: C. Gene**

---

# Core Concept

A **Gene** is the **basic unit of heredity**.

Genes carry **genetic information** that determines traits such as:

• Eye colour
• Height
• Blood group
• Hair type

Genes are segments of **DNA** located on **chromosomes**.

Hierarchy:

[
\text{Gene} \subset \text{DNA} \subset \text{Chromosome}
]

---

# How Heredity Works

1. Parents pass **chromosomes** to offspring.
2. Chromosomes contain **DNA**.
3. DNA contains **genes**.
4. Genes determine **traits**.

Thus the **smallest functional hereditary unit is the gene**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Chromosome ❌ (Incorrect)

A **Chromosome** carries many genes.

Example:

• Humans have **46 chromosomes (23 pairs)**.

Chromosomes **carry genes**, but they are **not the smallest hereditary unit**.

---

## B. DNA ❌ (Incorrect)

**DNA** is the molecule that stores genetic information.

However, heredity is determined by **specific segments of DNA called genes**.

Thus DNA is **larger than a gene**.

---

## C. Gene ✔ (Correct)

A gene is the **smallest unit responsible for heredity**.

Each gene controls a **specific trait**.

Example:

• Gene for eye colour
• Gene for blood type

Thus this is the correct answer.

---

## D. Nucleus ❌ (Incorrect)

The **cell nucleus** is the part of the cell that contains chromosomes.

But it is **not the hereditary unit itself**.

---

# Quick Comparison

| Structure | Function |
|---|---|
| Gene | Basic unit of heredity |
| DNA | Genetic molecule |
| Chromosome | Structure carrying many genes |
| Nucleus | Organelle containing chromosomes |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember this order:

[
\text{Gene} \rightarrow \text{DNA} \rightarrow \text{Chromosome} \rightarrow \text{Nucleus}
]

So the **unit of heredity = Gene**.

questions.text: Gene is
options: A segment of DNA and histone
A segment of DNA, RNA and histone
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: A segment of DNA**

A gene is a **specific sequence of DNA** that codes for a protein or functional RNA.

Option Analysis:
• Segment of DNA → Correct definition.
• DNA and histone → Histones help packaging but are not part of gene sequence.
• DNA, RNA and histone → Incorrect definition.
• All of the above → Incorrect.

questions.text: Who delivered the principle of ‘jumping gene’?
questions.solution:
This question is from **genetics and molecular biology**, which often appears in the Biology section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Who delivered the principle of “jumping gene”?**

A. Gregor Johann Mendel
B. Thomas Hunt Morgan
C. Barbara McClintock
D. Watson and Crick

✅ **Correct Answer: C. Barbara McClintock**

---

# Core Concept: Jumping Genes

**Jumping genes** are called **transposons** or **transposable elements**.

They are segments of DNA that can:

• **Move from one position to another within the genome**

This movement can affect gene expression and cause genetic variation.

The concept was discovered by **Barbara McClintock** while studying maize (corn).

She received the **Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1983** for this discovery.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Gregor Johann Mendel ❌ (Incorrect)

**Gregor Johann Mendel** discovered the **basic laws of inheritance**.

His contributions:

• Law of dominance
• Law of segregation
• Law of independent assortment

But he **did not discover jumping genes**.

---

## B. Thomas Hunt Morgan ❌ (Incorrect)

**Thomas Hunt Morgan** worked with **fruit flies (Drosophila)**.

His major contribution:

• **Chromosomal theory of inheritance**

He did not discover transposons.

---

## C. Barbara McClintock ✔ (Correct)

Barbara McClintock discovered **transposable genetic elements** in maize.

These genes can **move or “jump” within chromosomes**, hence the name **jumping genes**.

Thus this is the correct answer.

---

## D. Watson and Crick ❌ (Incorrect)

**James Watson** and **Francis Crick** discovered the **double helix structure of DNA in 1953**.

They did not discover jumping genes.

---

# Quick Summary of Contributions

| Scientist | Contribution |
|---|---|
| Mendel | Laws of inheritance |
| Morgan | Chromosomal theory |
| Barbara McClintock | Jumping genes (transposons) |
| Watson & Crick | DNA double helix structure |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember this association:

• **Mendel → inheritance laws**
• **Morgan → chromosome theory**
• **Watson & Crick → DNA structure**
• **Barbara McClintock → jumping genes**

Thus the correct answer is **Barbara McClintock**.

questions.text: Which compound present in D.N.A. does not form amino acid?
questions.solution:
This question is from **molecular biology (DNA and amino acids)**, a topic that often appears in the Biology section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Which compound present in DNA does not form amino acid?**

A. Adenine
B. Tyrosine
C. Guanine
D. Cytosine

✅ **Correct Answer: B. Tyrosine**

---

# Core Concept

DNA contains **four nitrogenous bases**:

• **Adenine (A)**
• **Guanine (G)**
• **Cytosine (C)**
• **Thymine (T)**

These bases form **genetic codes (codons)** that determine which **amino acids are produced during protein synthesis**.

However, **Tyrosine itself is an amino acid**, not a component of DNA.

Thus it is the compound **not present in DNA**.

---

# Structure of DNA

Each DNA nucleotide contains:

1. **Nitrogenous base**
2. **Deoxyribose sugar**
3. **Phosphate group**

Bases present in DNA:

| Purines | Pyrimidines |
|---|---|
| Adenine | Cytosine |
| Guanine | Thymine |

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Adenine ❌ (Incorrect)

**Adenine** is a **nitrogenous base present in DNA**.

It pairs with **thymine**.

Thus it is part of DNA and participates in coding for amino acids.

---

## B. Tyrosine ✔ (Correct)

**Tyrosine** is an **amino acid** used to build proteins.

It is **not a component of DNA**.

Therefore it is the correct answer.

---

## C. Guanine ❌ (Incorrect)

**Guanine** is another **nitrogenous base in DNA**.

It pairs with **cytosine**.

Thus it is part of DNA.

---

## D. Cytosine ❌ (Incorrect)

**Cytosine** is also a **DNA base**.

It pairs with **guanine**.

Thus it is present in DNA.

---

# Quick Comparison

| Substance | Type |
|---|---|
| Adenine | DNA base |
| Guanine | DNA base |
| Cytosine | DNA base |
| Tyrosine | Amino acid |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember:

**DNA bases = A, T, G, C**

• Adenine
• Thymine
• Guanine
• Cytosine

Anything outside these (like **Tyrosine**) is **not part of DNA**.

questions.text:
Which of the following professional(s) are more likely to run the risk of a permanent change in their cell’s DNA?
1. Researchers using carbon 14 isotope
2. X-ray technician
3. Coal miner
4. Dyer and painter
Select the correct answer by using the codes given below.
questions.solution:
This question is from **genetics (mutations and mutagens)**, which sometimes appears in the Biology section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

The question asks **which professions are more likely to experience permanent changes in DNA**.

A **permanent change in DNA** is called a **mutation**.

Mutations are usually caused by **mutagens**, such as:

• **Radiation** (X-rays, radioactive isotopes)
• **Chemical mutagens**
• **Certain industrial chemicals**

---

# Professions Given

1. Researchers using carbon-14 isotope
2. X-ray technician
3. Coal miner
4. Dyer and painter

We must check which ones are exposed to **mutagens**.

---

# Explanation of Each Case

## 1. Researchers using carbon-14 isotope ✔

Carbon-14 is a **radioactive isotope**.

Radioactive radiation can:

• Damage DNA
• Cause **mutations**

Therefore researchers handling **carbon-14** are at risk of DNA changes.

---

## 2. X-ray technician ✔

X-rays are **ionizing radiation**.

Ionizing radiation can:

• Break DNA strands
• Cause **genetic mutations**

Thus X-ray technicians are exposed to mutagenic radiation.

---

## 3. Coal miner ❌

Coal miners are mainly exposed to:

• Coal dust
• Methane gas
• Respiratory hazards

These mainly cause **lung diseases**, not direct DNA mutation from radiation.

Thus this option is usually **not included**.

---

## 4. Dyer and painter ✔

Dyers and painters may be exposed to **chemical mutagens**, such as:

• Industrial dyes
• Organic solvents
• Aromatic amines

These chemicals can cause **DNA mutations**.

---

# Correct Combination

✔ **1, 2 and 4**

Because they involve **radiation or chemical mutagens**.

---

# Key Concept

**Mutagens** that cause DNA mutation:

| Type | Examples |
|---|---|
| Physical | X-rays, UV rays, radioactive isotopes |
| Chemical | Dyes, solvents, carcinogens |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

If the profession involves:

• **Radiation**
• **Radioactive materials**
• **Industrial chemicals**

➡ Higher risk of **DNA mutation**.

Thus the correct answer is:

**1, 2 and 4**.

questions.text: Which of the following is a source of biofertilizer?
questions.solution:
This question is from **agricultural microbiology / biofertilizers**, a topic that appears in Biology for **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Which of the following is a source of biofertilizer?**

A. Yeast
B. Chlorella
C. Azolla
D. Mold

✅ **Correct Answer: C. Azolla**

---

# Core Concept

A **biofertilizer** is a substance containing **living microorganisms** that improve soil fertility by:

• Fixing atmospheric nitrogen
• Increasing nutrient availability to plants

Common biofertilizers include:

• Rhizobium
• Azotobacter
• Blue-green algae
• **Azolla**

---

# What is Azolla?

**Azolla** is a small floating aquatic fern.

It forms a **symbiotic relationship with the cyanobacterium Anabaena**.

Function:

• Fixes **atmospheric nitrogen**
• Enriches soil fertility

It is widely used in **paddy (rice) fields** as a natural biofertilizer.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Yeast ❌ (Incorrect)

**Yeast**

Uses:

• Baking
• Alcohol fermentation

Yeast mainly produces **carbon dioxide and alcohol**, not nitrogen fixation.

Thus it is **not a biofertilizer**.

---

## B. Chlorella ❌ (Incorrect)

**Chlorella**

Uses:

• Food supplement
• Oxygen production experiments

It is **not commonly used as a biofertilizer** in agriculture.

---

## C. Azolla ✔ (Correct)

Azolla contains **nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria**.

Benefits:

• Increases soil nitrogen
• Improves rice crop productivity

Thus it is a **biofertilizer source**.

---

## D. Mold ❌ (Incorrect)

**Mold**

Molds are mainly involved in:

• Decomposition
• Food spoilage

They are **not used as biofertilizers**.

---

# Quick Summary

| Organism | Main Use |
|---|---|
| Yeast | Fermentation |
| Chlorella | Nutritional supplement |
| Azolla | Biofertilizer in rice fields |
| Mold | Decomposition |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember this association:

**Rice fields → Azolla → Biofertilizer**

So the correct answer is **Azolla**.

questions.text: Which of the following is a biofertilizer?
questions.solution:
This question is from **biofertilizers and agricultural microbiology**, a topic that appears in the Biology section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Which of the following is a biofertilizer?**

A. Urea
B. 2,4-D
C. Streptocycline
D. Azolla

✅ **Correct Answer: D. Azolla**

---

# Core Concept: Biofertilizer

A **biofertilizer** is a substance containing **living microorganisms** that improve soil fertility by:

• Fixing atmospheric nitrogen
• Increasing nutrient availability to plants

Examples of biofertilizers:

• Rhizobium
• Azotobacter
• Blue-green algae
• **Azolla**

---

# Why Azolla is a Biofertilizer

**Azolla** is a small floating aquatic fern.

It has a **symbiotic relationship with the cyanobacterium Anabaena**, which:

• Fixes atmospheric nitrogen
• Enriches soil fertility

Because of this nitrogen-fixing ability, Azolla is widely used in **paddy (rice) fields**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Urea ❌ (Incorrect)

**Urea**

Urea is a **chemical fertilizer**, not a biofertilizer.

It directly supplies **nitrogen** to plants but does not contain living microorganisms.

---

## B. 2,4-D ❌ (Incorrect)

**2,4-D**

2,4-D (2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid) is a **weed killer (herbicide)**.

It is used to control unwanted plants, not to fertilize soil.

---

## C. Streptocycline ❌ (Incorrect)

**Streptocycline**

Streptocycline is an **antibiotic used in agriculture** to control bacterial diseases in plants.

It is **not a fertilizer**.

---

## D. Azolla ✔ (Correct)

Azolla contains **nitrogen-fixing microorganisms** and improves soil fertility.

Thus it is a **biofertilizer**.

---

# Quick Comparison

| Substance | Category |
|---|---|
| Urea | Chemical fertilizer |
| 2,4-D | Herbicide |
| Streptocycline | Antibiotic |
| Azolla | Biofertilizer |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

If the option is **a living organism used in agriculture**, it is likely a **biofertilizer**.

Example:

• **Azolla → Biofertilizer**
• **Rhizobium → Biofertilizer**
• **Blue-green algae → Biofertilizer**.

questions.text: The biofertilizer used as a supplementary food for the cattle, especially for milking bovinces is
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Azolla**

Azolla contains **20–25% protein** and is widely used as cattle feed.

questions.text: Which of these is not correctly matched?
questions.solution:
This question is from **agriculture biotechnology / biofertilizers and biological pest control**, which sometimes appears in the Biology section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

The question asks **which pair is not correctly matched**.

---

# Correct Answer

❌ **D. Azotobacter – Bioinsecticide**

This is incorrect because **Azotobacter is a biofertilizer, not a bioinsecticide**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Blue-green algae – Biofertilizer ✔ (Correct)

**Cyanobacteria**

Examples:

• Nostoc
• Anabaena

Function:

• Fix **atmospheric nitrogen**
• Improve soil fertility

Thus they are widely used as **biofertilizers in rice fields**.

---

## B. Chrysopa – Maho-control ✔ (Correct)

**Chrysopa**

Chrysopa (green lacewing) is a **beneficial insect** used in agriculture.

It feeds on pests like:

• Aphids (called **Maho** in some agricultural terminology)
• Small soft-bodied insects

Thus it is used for **biological pest control**.

---

## C. Bio-902 – Variety of mustard ✔ (Correct)

**Bio-902** is a **variety of mustard crop** developed for agriculture.

Thus this pair is correctly matched.

---

## D. Azotobacter – Bioinsecticide ❌ (Incorrect)

**Azotobacter**

Function:

• Fixes **atmospheric nitrogen**
• Improves soil fertility

Therefore it is a **biofertilizer**, not a **bioinsecticide**.

Hence this pair is **incorrectly matched**.

---

# Quick Summary

| Organism / Item | Correct Role |
|---|---|
| Blue-green algae | Biofertilizer |
| Chrysopa | Biological pest control |
| Bio-902 | Mustard variety |
| Azotobacter | Biofertilizer |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember common **biofertilizers**:

• Rhizobium
• Azotobacter
• Azospirillum
• Blue-green algae
• Azolla

If one of these is paired with **bioinsecticide**, it is likely **incorrect**.

---

You can also review **important biofertilizers, bioinsecticides, and crop varieties** frequently asked in competitive exams such as **DRDO, SSC, and agriculture-related papers**.

questions.text: Which bacteria is responsible for nitrogen fixation in soyabean?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Rhizobium japonicum**

This bacterium forms **root nodules in soybean plants** and fixes atmospheric nitrogen.

questions.text: 2, 4-D is
questions.solution:
This question is from **agricultural chemicals (pesticides and herbicides)**, a topic that sometimes appears in the Biology/General Science section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**2,4-D is**

A. An insecticide
B. An explosive
C. A fungicide
D. A herbicide

✅ **Correct Answer: D. A herbicide**

---

# Core Concept

**2,4-Dichlorophenoxyacetic acid**, commonly called **2,4-D**, is a **selective herbicide**.

Herbicides are chemicals used to **kill unwanted plants (weeds)**.

2,4-D specifically targets:

• **Broadleaf weeds**

while leaving **grasses and cereal crops** relatively unharmed.

---

# Why 2,4-D is Used

Farmers use 2,4-D to control weeds in crops such as:

• Wheat
• Rice
• Maize

It works by **disrupting plant growth hormones**, causing uncontrolled growth and death of weeds.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. An insecticide ❌ (Incorrect)

Insecticides are chemicals used to **kill insects**.

Examples:

• DDT
• Malathion

2,4-D does **not kill insects**, so this option is incorrect.

---

## B. An explosive ❌ (Incorrect)

Explosives are substances that **release energy rapidly**.

Examples:

• TNT
• RDX

2,4-D is an agricultural chemical, not an explosive.

---

## C. A fungicide ❌ (Incorrect)

Fungicides are used to **kill fungal pathogens** that cause plant diseases.

Examples:

• Copper sulfate
• Sulfur fungicides

2,4-D does not control fungi.

---

## D. A herbicide ✔ (Correct)

Herbicides are used to **destroy weeds**.

Since 2,4-D kills unwanted plants, it is classified as a **herbicide**.

---

# Quick Comparison

| Chemical Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Herbicide | Kills weeds |
| Insecticide | Kills insects |
| Fungicide | Kills fungi |
| Rodenticide | Kills rodents |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember these common examples:

• **2,4-D → Herbicide**
• **DDT → Insecticide**
• **Copper sulfate → Fungicide**

Thus the correct answer is **Herbicide**.

questions.text: Blue-green algae are chiefly used as biofertilizer in the crop of
questions.solution:
This question is from **biofertilizers and agricultural microbiology**, a topic sometimes asked in the Biology section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Blue-green algae are chiefly used as biofertilizer in the crop of**

A. Wheat
B. Gram
C. Paddy
D. Mustard

✅ **Correct Answer: C. Paddy**

---

# Core Concept

**Cyanobacteria** (blue-green algae) are microorganisms that can **fix atmospheric nitrogen**.

Examples:

• Anabaena
• Nostoc
• Aulosira

They convert atmospheric nitrogen into forms that plants can use, improving **soil fertility**.

---

# Why They Are Used in Paddy Fields

Rice (paddy) fields are usually **water-logged**, which creates ideal conditions for blue-green algae.

Benefits:

• Fix nitrogen naturally
• Increase soil fertility
• Reduce need for chemical fertilizers

Thus they are mainly used in **paddy cultivation**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Wheat ❌ (Incorrect)

Wheat is a **dryland crop** and is not grown in water-logged fields.

Blue-green algae grow best in **standing water**, so they are not mainly used for wheat.

---

## B. Gram ❌ (Incorrect)

Gram (chickpea) is a **leguminous plant**.

Nitrogen fixation in gram occurs mainly through **Rhizobium**, not blue-green algae.

---

## C. Paddy ✔ (Correct)

Paddy fields provide **moist aquatic conditions**, ideal for cyanobacteria growth.

Thus blue-green algae are widely used as **biofertilizers in rice cultivation**.

---

## D. Mustard ❌ (Incorrect)

Mustard is an **oilseed crop grown in dry soil**.

Blue-green algae are not typically used as fertilizers for mustard crops.

---

# Quick Summary

| Crop | Common Biofertilizer |
|---|---|
| Paddy | Blue-green algae |
| Legumes (gram, pea) | Rhizobium |
| Soybean | Rhizobium japonicum |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember this direct pair:

**Blue-green algae → Paddy (rice fields)**.

This combination appears frequently in competitive exams.

questions.text: Blue-green algae is used to supply nitrogen to
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Paddy**

Rice fields commonly use **cyanobacteria biofertilizers**.

questions.text: Which one of the following is not correctly matched?
questions.solution:
This question is from **biofertilizers and nitrogen fixation in agriculture**, a topic that appears in the Biology section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

The question is asking **which pair is not correctly matched**.

✅ **Correct Answer: D. Azolla – Maize**

This pair is incorrect because **Azolla is mainly used in paddy (rice) fields, not maize**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Blue-green algae – Paddy ✔ (Correct)

**Cyanobacteria** (blue-green algae) fix atmospheric nitrogen.

Examples:

• Anabaena
• Nostoc

They grow well in **water-logged conditions**, so they are commonly used as **biofertilizers in paddy (rice) fields**.

---

## B. Rhizobium – Pea ✔ (Correct)

**Rhizobium** lives in the **root nodules of leguminous plants**.

Examples of host plants:

• Pea
• Gram
• Lentil
• Bean

Thus **Rhizobium – Pea** is correctly matched.

---

## C. Azotobacter – Wheat ✔ (Correct)

**Azotobacter** fixes nitrogen in soil **without forming root nodules**.

It is commonly used for **non-leguminous crops**, such as:

• Wheat
• Rice
• Maize

Thus **Azotobacter – Wheat** is a correct match.

---

## D. Azolla – Maize ❌ (Incorrect)

**Azolla** is a small aquatic fern.

It contains the nitrogen-fixing cyanobacterium **Anabaena**.

Azolla is mainly used in **paddy fields**, not maize.

Therefore **Azolla – Maize** is incorrectly matched.

---

# Quick Summary

| Biofertilizer | Crop Association |
|---|---|
| Blue-green algae | Paddy |
| Rhizobium | Leguminous plants (pea, gram) |
| Azotobacter | Wheat and other non-legumes |
| Azolla | Paddy fields |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember these common pairs:

• **Blue-green algae → Paddy**
• **Rhizobium → Legumes**
• **Azotobacter → Wheat / non-legumes**
• **Azolla → Paddy**

If **Azolla is paired with maize**, it is **incorrect**.

questions.text: Azolla gives a good biofertilizer when mixed with
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Blue-green algae**

The combination enhances **nitrogen fixation efficiency**.

questions.text: Azolla is often used as biofertilizer because it is associated with
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Blue-green algae**

Azolla contains **Anabaena azollae**, a nitrogen-fixing cyanobacterium.
